responses.GS(2): The Project Area does not reside within a USDW zone. This assessment is based on the 1982 MOA and the Class II primacy application, the hydrocarbon-bearing zones within the productive limits of named oil fields, as documented in the former DOG “California Oil and Gas Fields” volumes are accepted as exempt aquifers under 40 CFR 146.4 (US EPA, 1982). In the Project Area, the target injection interval consists of the Kione and Forbes Formations, both which are named in California Oil and Gas Fields Volume 3.

responses.ES(1): The average ZEI calculated per well is 316’ which is much less than a quarter-mile radius; therefore, the conservative approach of using a quarter-mile radius around the injectors was used to define the AOR. ZEI calculations can be found under the uploaded document ES(1)_ZEI_Calculation. Reservoir properties used for calculations are referenced in the reservoir characteristics table under GS(1) section of the Geologic Study.

responses.ES(4): No drilling or plugging-and-abandonment programs required to complete this project. All idle wells will be tested or abandoned per the appropriate California Geologic Energy Management (CalGEM) Division regulations. Abandoned wells have been plugged back to meet CalGEM requirements at the time of abandonment. These wells will not have an adverse effect on the project or cause damage to life, health and property.
responses.IP(1): The primary purpose is to dispose of produced water from Sacramento Basin gas fields into the Kione and Forbes formations using well Section 58 Unit 18[cite: 944, 945].
responses.IP(3): The project is expected to last 30 years with a maximum injection rate of 600 bwpd and a cumulative volume of 6,570 Mbbls[cite: 953, 954].
responses.IP(5): Monitoring includes annual fluid injection profile surveys, casing-tubing annulus pressure tests every five years, and continuous SCADA monitoring of pressures and rates[cite: 964, 965, 967].
responses.IP(8): Injection fluid is produced water exclusively sourced from gas production wells within the Sacramento basin[cite: 986].
responses.OI(1): The Maximum Allowable Surface Injection Pressure (MASP) is determined to be 1,623 psi based on a fracture gradient of 0.81 psi/ft[cite: 1171, 1173].
responses.CL(1): Consistent with the Underground Injection Control (UIC) Geologic Energy Management Division Project Information Checklist, dated April 8, 2020, Item CP(1), California Resources Corporation has determined that the project submission is subject to the requirements of California Business and Professions Code sections 6735, 7835, and 7835.1, and as such, the licensed professionals identified below are responsible for all data, interpretations, and calculations that are within the scope of their respective professional licenses.
responses.PO(1): California Resources Corporation is requesting the approval of a Class II Water Disposal Project for the Kione and Forbes Formations in Willows-Beehive Bend Gas Field. The primary purpose of the project is to inject excess produced water from CRC's gas production facilities into the Kione and Forbes Formations within the EPA-approved aquifer exemption boundary (1983). The project will consist of converting one (1) Class II disposal well within the proposed UIC Project Area, which defined as being on CRC-leased property that is on the east flank of the Willows-Beehive Bend anticline, east of Hwy 5, and within the aquifer exemption boundary (Figure 1). The Project Area includes approximately 160 acres or 0.25 square miles.
responses.ES(5):
In December 2025, California Resources Corporation conducted an electronic search to identify the location of water wells within 1-mile of the proposed UIC project boundary. The data from the DWR and GAMA systems are incomplete, and the well types are not specified. The latitude and longitude coordinates are approximate, so the well locations shown on the Department of Water Resources
(DWR) database and the California State Water Resources Control Board Groundwater Ambient Monitoring Assessment Program (GAMA) site maps are imprecise. For wells that included alphanumeric identifiers, the California well numbering system was used for the well locations instead plotting them in the center of the sections.
ES(2) and ES(3) contain a map of the water wells identified as well as Information including type and status for water supply wells.

responses.PO(1):
The primary purpose of Underground Injection Control (UIC) Project 46400396 is to enhance hydrocarbon
recovery by utilizing continuous and cyclic steam injection into the Tulare Formation located on the Main Camp
Lease (Sec 24 T31S R22E MDBM) of the Midway-Sunset Field (Kern County, California), as shown in Figure 1.
The Main Camp Lease is currently operated by Berry Corporation (Berry). The current Project Approval Letter
(PAL) was approved on July 30, 2013.
The current project consists of 67 wells, including 30 steamflood, 5 cyclic, and 32 oil and gas (OG) wells. Berry
is requesting an expansion of UIC Project 46400396 to incorporate the following:
1. 63 new wells (continuous injection and cyclic)
2. converting 26 of the 32 OG producers into cyclic steam status
Upon approval, the project will contain a total of 124 cyclic/steamflood injection wells under the UIC. Berry is
also requesting an update to the PAL to increase the total number of injection wells to 124 and increase the
permitted injection volume to 62,000 BSPD. This development plan is necessary to enhance the ultimate
hydrocarbon recovery of the lease, as operated by Berry.

responses.ES(1):
CalGEM requires an AOR to be completed prior to permitting a Class II injector within the UIC program as per
the federal Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) regulations. Pursuant to this requirement, Regulation 40 CFR 146.6
states the minimum fixed radius for an AOR is “1/4 mile from the wellbore unless an approved mathematical
model is used to determine the Zone of Endangering Influence (ZEI).” In accordance with the regulation, a ZEI
calculation was performed for proposed and existing wells (cyclic and continuous injection) using the Neuman
equation (Neuman, 1985).
The ZEI radius for a cyclic-steam producer is calculated to be 220 feet. Most of the heat and fluid injected
during a steam cycle is produced by the same well after it is returned to production. The calculation assumes
that each producer will be cycled twice a year, each year, for a period of 10 years for a total of 20 cycles for the
life of the well. It was further assumed that each cycle will inject 10,000 barrels of steam (Cold Water
Equivalent [CWE]). The cumulative total resultant steam injection volume in a vertical cyclic-steam producer
is calculated to be 200,000 barrels of steam (CWE), representing the maximum cumulative cycle-steam
stimulation injection volume expected per well. The volume is a high estimate for total injected volumes, as
the number of steam cycles performed on cyclic wells is dependent upon response of continuous steamflood
in the reservoir, thus the ZEI is considered as a conservative (i.e. large) estimate.
The ZEI radius for the typical continuous-steam-injector was calculated to be 318 feet. It was estimated that a
typical well will inject a total of 3,192,000 barrels of steam (CWE) into the targeted reservoir sands over a period
of 25 years. The maximum instantaneous steam injection rate per well is estimated to be 500 barrels of steam
(CWE) per day, per well decreasing to 50 barrels of steam (CWE) towards the end of life of the well as seen in
Graph A. The maximum injection rate of 500 barrels of steam per day (BSPD), per well (CWE) represents the
maximum rate of steam to be injected into a typical continuous injection well, while the 50 BSPD end-rate
represents the lowest rate of steam to be injected into a continuous injection well. The 500 barrels of steam
per day (CWE) injection rate would be sustained for approximately one year after which steam injection would
start to be ramped down over time. The ZEI radius for the continuous steam injector and cyclic steam
producer over the corresponding injection periods are reflected in Graph B. The combined ZEI radii for the
proposed and existing continuous steam injectors and cyclic-steam producer are shown in Figure 14 – Project
Development Map.

responses.IP(1):
The ZEI radius for the typical continuous-steam-injector was calculated to be 318 feet. It was estimated that a
typical well will inject a total of 3,192,000 barrels of steam (CWE) into the targeted reservoir sands over a period
of 25 years. The average steam-injection-rate per well is estimated to be equivalent to 350 barrels of steam
(CWE) per day, decreasing to 50 barrels of steam (CWE) over the life of the well.
responses.IP(2): The list of all wells within project is included in Appendix I- IP(4).
responses.IP(3):
The source of the steam injectate is produced water from the Tulare Formation. A condensed version of recent
geochemical analysis of water samples collected on February 18, 2025 across Berry’s Midway-Sunset assets
is included in Appendix K – IP(8) Injectate Analysis. That document indicate TDS values is 4700 mg/L from
the Tulare at Main Camp (Table 4).
responses.IP(4):
The method of injection for this project is continuous steamflood, a thermal enhanced oil recovery process,
via dedicated continuous steam injector wells. Steam is injected into the reservoir through dedicated tubing
strings below thermal packers. Bottom-hole assembly consists of high-temperature thermal packers which
are set above the injection zone (Figure 17a). This assembly creates an isolation between the casing annulus
and tubing to prevent vertical steam migration. In addition to continuous steam injection, cyclic steam
stimulation (CSS) may be applied to select wells as an initial step to improve injectivity or as a supplemental
recovery technique. Injection wells are equipped with a pressure gauge and monitored daily to ensure MASP
is not exceeded (Figure 17b).
responses.IP(5):
Cathodic protection is in place on the DOT gas line providing fuel gas for generators. No other cathodic
protection measures for the facility, lines or wells are planned at this time, since no significant corrosion has
been identified within the project area.
responses.IP(6):
The Area of Review analysis for the steamflood and cyclic steam project identified 12 wells operated by Berry
within the ZEI Boundary, which do not meet the current CalGEM regulatory requirements for zonal isolation of
the intended injection zone (Title 14- California Code of Regulations, Section 1724.7). The cement calculations
used to screen for potential conduit wells and the potential conduit wells identified are listed within in
Appendix J – IP(5) Cement Calculations and Potential Conduit Wells. This list of wells includes all potential
conduits that fall within this project’s AOR boundary, along with a summary of their current status and a brief
description of how Berry intends to remediate the issue(s). The second list contains wells that will have the
lower portion of their completion interval plugged back upon approval of this project-by-project review.
There has been no indication of steam migration out of zone to date. Berry is proposing a monitoring program
to further ensure the injectate remains confined to the permitted zones. The proposed monitoring plan
consists of the following elements with data submissions to CALGEM as required:
1. Mechanical Integrity Testing (Parts One and Two) in alignment with UIC Regulations 1724.10.2 and
1724.10.3. These regulations stipulate a variety of well integrity and injection monitoring techniques
(temperature surveys, radioactive tracer surveys, casing pressure tests, etc.) that will identify
injection anomalies.
2. Idle well testing according to UIC Regulations 1772.1 that ensures idle wells are mechanically
sound, preventing migration of fluid into non permitted zones. Any indication of steam out zone will
Volume 1 Page 12 of 35
UIC Project 46400396
be followed by additional surveillance and re- abandonment as necessary. This plan adequately
addresses any zonal isolation concerns and monitoring of the project.
3. Idle well fluid levels will be gathered as needed to ensure the wells in question have maintained
mechanical integrity.
4. In addition, Berry will run temperature logs on wells identified as having potential conduits until
remedial actions are completed. These temperature logs will be conducted annually, and any
anomalies observed outside the zone of injection will trigger an escalation of the planned
remediation.
responses.SI(3):
No known water supply well(s) were identified within 500 feet of the UIC Project boundary (AOR radius
boundary).
responses.OI(1):
MASP Determination
The calculated Maximum Allowable Surface Injection Pressure (MASIP) is calculated via the following
equation:
MASIP= (0.95*IG-IFG) * TVD
Where IG is the injection gradient of 0.6 psi/ft
IFG is the injected fluid gradient of 0.02 psi/ft
TVD is the uppermost perforation depth in ft.
The calculated Injection Gradient is multiplied by 0.95 to appropriately account for factors such as a
conservative allowance for friction loss, in accordance with Section 1724.10.3- Maximum Allowable Surface
Injection Pressure.
Injection wells pressure will not exceed its permitted limit for this project. Calculated MASP for wells is
included in Appendix L – OI(1) MASP Calculations.
responses.OI(2):
Letters to Offset Operators
Copies of letters of notification to offset operators can be found in Appendix M – OI(2) Offset Operator
Letters.

responses.PO(1):
Aera Energy LLC (Aera Energy, Aera), a subsidiary of California Resources Corporation, is requesting a review of Underground Injection Control (UIC) Class II Water Injection Project(s) with the intent to consolidate all Aera operated active water injection projects for the Pico Formation in the Ventura Avenue Oil Field (Ventura Field). The primary purpose of these waterflood injection projects is to provide injection support to the hydrocarbon producing sands located in the Ventura Field.
All active and inactive injection wells are contained within the existing UIC project(s). Currently, Aera Energy recognizes 18 active UIC projects; however, this project review is to consolidate three projects into one. The consolidation projects include 8140012, 8140016, and 8140024, which are all within the T410 fault block discussed throughout this project review document and highlighted in the regional map of the Ventura Oil Field in
Figure 1
. These projects are also listed in
Table 1
. This project review has been prepared following the project data requirements specified in the California Code of Regulations, Title 14, Section 1724.7 and the format outlined in the UIC Project Data Requirements Checklist from the Department of Conservation, California Geologic Energy Management Division (CalGEM).

responses.ES(1):
The project area of review was determined by plotting all existing and future development Diatomite cyclic well locations on a map. Then, one ZEI was calculated for all locations utilizing the Neuman method and the associated input values for a “Diatomite Representative Cyclic Producer”.  The properties for a “Diatomite Representative Cyclic Producer” were derived by evaluating all existing cyclic producers in the project area, which are also analogous for future development Diatomite cyclic wells. 
The Neuman method calculates a circular steam zone area as the ZEI. The steam zone area as a function of time is calculated using the following equation:
A(t)=(x_SQ*(m_s ) ̇*5.615*ρ_w*L_v*√(αt/π)*〖(1-x〗_HP))/(k_hr*(T_s-T_r )*43,560)

The input variable ranges for a representative diatomite cyclic producer are presented in Table ES-1-1. Detailed calculations are provided in Appendix ES-1 (ZEI Spreadsheet). 
The calculated individual ZEI for a representative cyclic producer is 82 feet, corresponding to an area of 0.48 acres.  This calculation assumes a maximum cyclic steam injection rate of 2,000 barrels per day cold-water-equivalent (bbl/d-cwe) per completed interval, injected at 90% steam quality. Each injection cycle consists of a five-day injection period, followed by a two-day soak period, and a production phase lasting approximately 14 to 21 days, or until the well ceases to flow.
Under these operating assumptions, each well would undergo approximately 12 to 15 injection cycles per year over a 10-year period. Based on individual steam cycles of approximately 10,000 bbl-cwe, the maximum cumulative injected steam per completed interval over 10 years is estimated to be 1,500,000 bbl-cwe.
The resulting ZEI is larger than that derived from fracture design data. The Area of Review for the project was established by combining the individual ZEIs associated with all existing and proposed cyclic producers within the project area.
responses.PO(1):
Aera Energy LLC (Aera) puts forth this project with a primary purpose to inject steam into the Miocene-aged Reef Ridge Diatomite at the subject Sections in the Belridge Field (Township 28S, Range 21E).  The proposed project is in an area where the target injection zones are not an Underground Source of Drinking Water (USDW).  The project entails drilling 1329 cyclic steam producers and cyclic operations on an existing 64 wells.


Figure ES-2-1 below contains items (1) an (4). To maintain map legibility, wells within the project area are posted on these maps, as well as wells within 300 feet of the project area. Any further posting would make the maps illegible. For better readability, higher resolution maps may be found in Appendix ES-2-1. Items (2) and (3) do not exist in the project area.
responses.ES(5):
In February 2026,   Aera conducted an electronic search to identify the location of water wells within 1 mile of the UIC project boundary. The data sources utilized include the Department of Water Resources (DWR) well completion reports (WCR), the State Water Resources Control Board - Groundwater Ambient Monitoring and Assessment (GAMA) information system, and the USGS. These data were filtered to include wells within 1 mile of the UIC project boundary and eliminated destroyed wells, monitoring wells, and cathodic protection wells. The data from the DWR and GAMA systems are incomplete, and the well types are not specified. The latitude and longitude coordinates are approximate, so the well locations shown on the DWR and GAMA site maps are imprecise. For wells that included alphanumeric identifiers, the California well numbering system was used for the well locations instead plotting them in the center of the sections.
There are a total of 21 wells located within 1 mile of the UIC project boundary. The information for these wells is tabulated in Appendix W-ES-6 and displayed on Figure W-ES-6-1. The majority of the wells are monitoring wells associated with oil field activities. There are two industrial use wells identified in Section 34, T28S 21E. The status of all wells is not available. Historical and current land use of the area would indicate that the remaining unknown well types are likely industrial supply use rather than irrigation or domestic use. A higher resolution map may also be found in Appendix W-ES-6.

responses.GS(2):
Representative viscosity and API gravity data from five wells are included  in Table GS-2-1 below. It is important to note that this is raw, lab-derived data. Therefore, it is not completely reflective of the actual viscosity and API of subsurface oil due to the inevitable loss of lighter-end oil while retrieving the sample. Therefore, these values represent higher viscosity and lower API than actual reservoir conditions. Corrections can be applied to account for the loss of the lighter-end oil, hence why the average values for viscosity and API shown in Table GS-2-2 are higher than the ones shown below.

responses.IP(4):
Aera employs a robust well design fit-for-purpose for Diatomite cyclic steaming.  Some of the details of this design include: 
•	Surface casing to ~450 feet 
•	7” L-80 production casing with premium casing connections MO-4ST  
•	Perforated or frac-pack completion with wire-wrapped screen. Frac pack is only utilized if higher solids production is expected (Frac pack completion is for solids control not for productivity improvement) 
•	Premium wellhead and components rated to 600oF 
•	Haliburton premium "Well Life " high temperature cement blend  
For wells with frac-pack (fracturing and gravel packing) completion designs, a hydraulic fracture is created in the rock and an Inconel 825 wire-wrapped screen is set across the fractured completion interval to hydraulically isolate the perforation interval and minimize solids inflow into the well.  Wells with frac-pack completions require WST permits to accomplish the frac-pack portion of the well completion procedure.  Subsequent steaming operations are through the frac-packed Inconel wire-wrapped screen.    
This casing design has proven to be effective in the Diatomite cyclic steaming operations in Belridge East Flank.  Because of the success in initial well completions and continuing operation, no significant changes to this design are planned in the future.  A generic South Belridge East Flank cyclic wellbore diagram is shown in






Figure IP-6-1: Generic East Flank Cyclic Wellbore Diagram (Gravel-pack completion)
responses.IP(6):
For surveillance, each Cyclic well in the project area has digital pressure and flow rate meters, in addition to an automated flow valve programmed to keep injection pressure below the MASIP (Maximum allowable surface injection pressure). The data is available to remotely monitor in real time and is aggregated into a daily average pressure and injection rate.  Daily injection pressure records are kept for the lifetime of the injector. A daily surveillance process flags cyclic wells with abnormal behavior for further evaluation.  


Mechanical Integrity Testing (MIT I and II) will occur as required by the CalGEM to ensure steam injected is confined to the approved zone(s) of injection. If there is evidence a well lacks mechanical integrity CalGEM will be notified, and the well will be disconnected from the injection system. 
CRC is also committed to implement active tiltmeter monitoring and enhanced wellbore surveillance as part of the project. Tiltmeter arrays will be installed to monitor for surface deformation associated with dilation resulting from pressure, temperature, or fluid accumulation during injection operations. In addition, periodic temperature logging will be conducted in selected observation and offset wells to identify any anomalous heat signatures indicative of steam or fluid migration beyond the approved injection zone. These monitoring measures will provide real-time and diagnostic data to verify fracture containment and ensure early detection of any out-of-zone growth or unintended fluid movement.
INSAR (Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar) is another tool Aera uses to monitor ground movement to help manage subsidence or dilation. This data is received every 24 days as satellites fly over the Belridge field. The INSAR data has historically shown little ground movement in the East Flank cyclic area. Figure IP-5-1 shows the high, average, and low subsidence rates in inches per year on the Y-axis and time on the X-axis. As can be seen in the graph, subsidence has been well controlled over the last 10 years near the cyclic area. Aera attempts to keep subsidence in the range of 5 to –5 inches per year to minimize risk of shear failures.



Figure IP-5-1: East Flank Cyclic area subsidence history (Feb-01 through Feb-20)
 

Fracture Parameters and Vertical Stand Off
Fracture containment within the approved Diatomite injection zone has been verified using field-measured tiltmeter mapping data from 45 South Belridge wells.  
Tiltmeter monitoring provides direct measurements of fracture geometry, including azimuth, depth, and vertical height growth during injection. Results show that upward fracture growth from the top perforation in the upper Opal A stages averages approximately 10 feet, with a maximum observed average of 40 feet, as shown in Figure IP-5-2.  
Comparison of mapped fracture tops to the geologic Top of Diatomite confirms that all fractures remain below the Top of Diatomite, maintaining positive vertical stand-off. These field measurements demonstrate that fracture propagation is contained within the approved injection interval and does not extend outside the injection zone. 
Figure IP-5-2: Top of Diatomite to Top of Fracture Distance 
 

In addition, Aera performed net pressure matching analyses on more than 300 wells to independently evaluate fracture geometry and vertical growth. Net pressure modeling provides analytical estimates of fracture height and extent based on injection pressures and formation mechanical properties. Results indicate that the modeled fracture top distance did not exceed the Top of the stimulation formation. As shown in Figure IP-5-3, the calculated separation between the fracture top and the formation top remains positive across the dataset, confirming vertical containment within the approved injection interval. These analytical results are consistent with the field-measured tiltmeter data and further demonstrate that fracture propagation does not extend beyond the injection zone.



Figure IP-5-3: Top of Fracture to Top of Formation from Net Pressure Match
 

An independent study was performed by Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (LLNL) to investigate risk factors for surface expressions. LLNL also noted, “The South Belridge study area has deep diatomite and deep injection. No surface expressions have emerged in this area” (LLNL-TR-819790 P. 72).
The high pressure cyclic steam (HPCS) diatomite formation at Belridge is considered “deep” where all current active wells are perforated deeper than 1,000 feet of true vertical depth (TVD) from ground elevation to top perforation. In addition, there are several stratigraphic intervals present, such as, the San Joaquin/Etchegion (undifferentiated) and the Tulare Formation, between the zone of injection (i.e. the diatomite) and surface. All proposed wells are also considered “deep” diatomite being 700 feet of TVD.
LLNL also summarizes that “surface expressions are expected to be connected to either cumulative or immediate injection intensity, a simple connection, particularly a connection to the injection intensity in the vicinity of the surface expressions, cannot be established in a definitive way” (LLNL-TR-819790 P. 84). Therefore, increasing or decreasing injection intensity in no way explains surface expression causation. The injection intensity in Belridge Diatomite HPCS would be considered as “low” injection intensity by comparison to other operators. The proposed future HPCS wells would operate under the same injection intensity as our current operating wells but have room to increase injection intensity as more heat is needed early on.
responses.IP(5):
Aera Energy’s first priority is safety for people and the environment.  Policies and guidelines are in place and the staff are fully committed to implement such policies. The corrosion inhibition plan starts at the well level where corrosion inhibition chemicals are applied in selected wells to protect the casing and downhole equipment from corrosion. Produced fluids are pumped to a dehydration facility where the oil is separated from water and custody transfer occurs at the sales meter. A portion of the produced water is then cleaned further and softened for the purpose of steam generation while the remaining water is transported throughout the water disposal system. The soft water distribution system is constructed mainly of bare carbon steel pipelines. A corrosion inhibition chemical is added in order to protect the bare carbon steel from corrosion. 
Aera Pipeline Management Plan has been prepared pursuant to CCR Title 8 - Section 6533.
Aera's inspection program for all service classes is that as part of routine field and facility surveillance, all pipelines are visually inspected for evidence of leakage, clamps, or excessive corrosion.
Documentation of inspections is provided on an "exception-only" basis, meaning that work orders for piping repair or replacement will be the form of inspection documentation to satisfy Title 14 Ch. 4, Section 1777.3. Aera Belridge, Cymric/McKittrick, and Lost Hills compiles type, grade, and design information and stores it in a computer-based data management system on a limited set of regulated pipelines (including the pipes tested to represent the service class) but not all lines greater than 1" nominal diameter. NDE Testing on any line designated as an Environmentally Sensitive Gathering Line greater than 10 years old or Urban pipeline over 4" in diameter and greater than 10 years old or active gas pipelines in sensitive areas greater than 10 years old per Title 14 Ch. 4, Section 1774.1 (f) shall be completed every two years regardless of Class.
Aera’s testing program varies by service class:
•	Class 1: Thickness monitoring inspections are performed on a representative sample of the service class. Inspections are completed for each representative line at 5 years or half (1/2) the remaining life, whichever is less. In cases where the remaining life is estimated to be less than 4 years, the inspection interval may be the full remaining life up to 2 years maximum and the number of piping systems inspected shall be increased within that class to detect worst case deterioration rates.
•	Class 2: Thickness monitoring inspections are performed on a representative sample of the service class. Inspections are completed for each representative line at 10 years or half (1/2) the remaining life, whichever is less.
•	Class 3: Thickness monitoring inspections are performed on a representative sample of the service class. Inspections are completed for each representative line at 15 years or three quarters (3/4) the remaining life, whichever is less.
•	Class 4: Piping systems within this class are visually inspected annually and tested by exception only, including pressure testing performed in accordance with Title 14, Ch. 4 Section 1774.1 (c).

 
Pipeline Management Plan
Field: Belridge, Cymric/McKittrick, and Lost Hills
